cards.prompt: A cascading fault drops main power. In the dark, the whole station hums with a low tone that matches the contact's rhythm exactly. Emergency reserves will last minutes.

leftChoice.text: Dump reserves into the lights and life support

rightChoice.text: Reroute power to boost the sensor array

cards.prompt: Sonar finally identifies the contact: not a creature, but a derelict autonomous rig — and behind it, the resupply sub, disabled and adrift, its crew alive inside. Your instruments clear as the interference peaks and fades.

leftChoice.text: Spend your last air venting to nudge them to your dock

rightChoice.text: Guide them by the mapped light-rhythm

cards.prompt: The interference lifts like a held breath released. The trench falls silent, the resupply airlock cycles green, and dawn-blue light from the surface elevators floods the dark corridors of Fathom Station. You held the deep at bay — and understood a little of what it was saying.

leftChoice.text: Log the anomaly and request rotation home

rightChoice.text: Volunteer to command the next descent

name: Ivory Tower
description: Steer an ancient university through scandal, funding wars and the discovery of a lifetime.
systemInstruction: You are a creative storyteller for the interactive fiction game "SwipeVerse". You create challenging scenarios themed as "Ivory Tower". The stats are Standing, Endowment, Students & Faculty, Scholarship.

deck.name: Chancellor of the Ivory Tower
deck.description: As the new chancellor of a proud but broke university, you juggle a vain donor, a plagiarism scandal, an occupied quad, and a librarian's astonishing discovery.

cards.prompt: On your first morning, a billionaire donor offers a fortune—if you rename the historic Hall of Founders after him. The endowment is bleeding; his check would stop the bleeding.

leftChoice.text: Accept the gift, rename the hall

rightChoice.text: Decline; heritage isn't for sale

cards.prompt: The renamed hall triggers uproar—the donor made his fortune in ways alumni find distasteful. Reporters ask if you vetted him.

rightChoice.text: Add a review clause, hedge quietly

cards.prompt: A junior scholar accuses a beloved history professor of lifting whole chapters from an obscure dissertation. The proof is thin but damning.

rightChoice.text: Bury it to protect the department

cards.prompt: Students pitch tents across the quad, demanding tuition relief and a voice on the board. Camera crews arrive by noon.

rightChoice.text: Order the quad cleared

cards.prompt: The head librarian, breathless, produces a water-stained folio: an unknown draft that may rewrite the founding history of the university itself.

rightChoice.text: Auction it discreetly to collectors

cards.prompt: The faculty union threatens to strike over frozen salaries and swelling class sizes.

leftChoice.text: Grant raises you can't quite afford

rightChoice.text: Offer titles and perks instead of cash

cards.prompt: Your chief financial officer lays out the numbers: the deficit is worse than the trustees know. Something must give.

rightChoice.text: Protect academics, defer maintenance

cards.prompt: Physics and Classics both claim the same windfall bequest. Each dean is certain the money is theirs.

rightChoice.text: Award it to Classics' rare-texts project

cards.prompt: A rival university dangles a lavish offer before your Nobel-track researcher. Losing her would gut the sciences.

leftChoice.text: Match the offer with a new institute

rightChoice.text: Let her go, keep the money

cards.prompt: The plagiarism story breaks nationally, tangled with the tuition protests. The university trends for the wrong reasons.

rightChoice.text: Spin it and blame a rogue actor

cards.prompt: The board of trustees, spooked by headlines, murmurs of a no-confidence vote in your first term.

leftChoice.text: Rally allies and dominate the meeting

rightChoice.text: Offer reforms and share power

cards.prompt: Whispers spread that the miraculous folio might be a clever forgery. A definitive test would be costly—and could humiliate you.

rightChoice.text: Celebrate it now, question it never

cards.prompt: An investment manager promises to triple returns on the endowment through an aggressive fund. The board is intrigued and nervous.

leftChoice.text: Wager a large slice of the endowment

rightChoice.text: Keep the endowment conservative

cards.prompt: Graduate students, underpaid and overworked, petition for livable stipends and threaten to withhold teaching.

rightChoice.text: Freeze pay, promise future review

cards.prompt: Engineers condemn the crumbling old sciences building. Classes must move or the roof might not last the winter.

rightChoice.text: Shutter it and cram students elsewhere

cards.prompt: Furious at your handling of the scandal, the naming donor threatens to rescind his entire pledge.

rightChoice.text: Return the gift with your dignity

cards.prompt: The provost warns that accreditors are coming, and their report could define the university's next decade.

rightChoice.text: Stage a glossy showcase visit

cards.prompt: A national foundation offers a transformative grant—but demands control over what your researchers may publish.

leftChoice.text: Take the money, accept the leash

rightChoice.text: Refuse; guard academic freedom

cards.prompt: The folio, now authenticated, could crown a public exhibition or fetch a fortune from a private buyer. The faculty and the ledger disagree.

leftChoice.text: Display it as the university's soul

rightChoice.text: Sell it to secure the future

cards.prompt: At convocation, you rise before students, faculty, trustees, and press. The ancient university has survived your first year—now you tell them what it stands for.

rightChoice.text: Pledge stability and growth

name: The Long Winter
description: Rule as jarl through a winter that will be remembered — the only question is in which saga.
systemInstruction: You are a creative storyteller for the interactive fiction game "SwipeVerse". You create challenging scenarios themed as "The Long Winter". The stats are Renown, Silver, The Clan, The Runes.

deck.name: The Long Winter
deck.description: A jarl must shepherd a divided great hall through the cruelest winter in living memory, weighing renown, silver, kinship, and the old runes against starvation and feud.

cards.prompt: Snow chokes the fjord and the raid season's spoils were thin. Your steward Halli spreads his hands over the half-empty stores and begs for a decision.

rightChoice.text: Keep the feasts to hold morale

cards.prompt: Across the longfire, the Bjornssons and Ravnssons glare. An old blood feud smolders under your one roof.

rightChoice.text: Force them to share a bench

cards.prompt: The völva scatters bone-runes across a hide and goes pale. 'Blood on the snow before the thaw,' she whispers.

rightChoice.text: Dismiss the old woman's dread

cards.prompt: Father Anselm, a christian missionary caught by the storm, kneels and asks to overwinter beneath your roof.

leftChoice.text: Grant him shelter for his coin

rightChoice.text: Cast him back into the cold

cards.prompt: A blizzard buries the hall to its eaves. Doors will not open and the drifts keep rising.

rightChoice.text: Wait it out and burn stores

cards.prompt: A merchant knarr sits frozen in the fjord ice, its shivering crew begging for warmth by your fire.

leftChoice.text: Shelter them and claim their cargo

rightChoice.text: Honor the guest-code, take nothing

cards.prompt: Crammed together, a Ravnsson spills ale on a Bjornsson's boots. Knives leap from sheaths at the longfire.

rightChoice.text: Let them settle it as men

cards.prompt: Grain vanishes from the locked store. A trembling thrall is dragged before you, protesting innocence.

rightChoice.text: Pardon him and seek the true thief

cards.prompt: Hunger gnaws. Scouts spy elk beyond the thin lake ice, a feast that could break the famine or the hunters.

leftChoice.text: Lead the hunt across the ice

rightChoice.text: Stay safe and eat the stores

cards.prompt: Young Ulf, hot-blooded, demands you sanction a raid on a neighbor's fat storehouse.

rightChoice.text: Forbid such dishonor

cards.prompt: A wet, wracking cough spreads bench to bench. The sick spit blood into the rushes.

rightChoice.text: Let the völva tend them

cards.prompt: The völva says the old ways demand a midwinter blót to feed the gods through the dark.

rightChoice.text: Refuse and save the beasts

cards.prompt: Some of your kin, swayed by the missionary, now refuse to join the blót and pray to a nailed god.

rightChoice.text: Drive them back to the old gods

cards.prompt: Rival Jarl Sigurd sends a rider: an alliance sealed by marriage, if you can meet the bride-price in silver.

rightChoice.text: Refuse the entanglement

cards.prompt: In the dead of night a hearth spark catches the thatch. Fire climbs the rafters as sleepers wake screaming.

rightChoice.text: Save the silver, risk the sleepers

cards.prompt: The feud draws blood at last: a Bjornsson lies dead, a Ravnsson blade still warm. Both kindreds demand your judgement.

leftChoice.text: Set a weregild, blood-price in silver

rightChoice.text: Exile the killer into the snow

cards.prompt: A crack echoes across the fjord as the ice begins to break. A trader's sledge appears on the first thaw-road.

rightChoice.text: Hoard your silver for lean days

cards.prompt: The exile's kin, shamed, rise in open revolt within the hall, torches in hand.

rightChoice.text: Reconcile them over a feast

cards.prompt: On the last long night of winter the völva casts the runes a final time, asking how the hall shall be remembered.

leftChoice.text: Trust the runes and hold a great feast

rightChoice.text: Trust your own arm, not the bones

cards.prompt: Meltwater sings from the eaves and green shows on the hillsides. Your hall endured the longest winter in living memory, and the skalds already sing of it.

leftChoice.text: Give thanks to the old gods

rightChoice.text: Raise your horn to the surviving clan

name: High Noon
description: Wear the star in a frontier town where the railroad, the ranchers and the outlaws all think they own it.
systemInstruction: You are a creative storyteller for the interactive fiction game "SwipeVerse". You create challenging scenarios themed as "High Noon". The stats are The Law, Gold, The Town, Frontier-Craft.

deck.name: High Noon at End-of-Track
deck.description: As the green sheriff of a rail-end boomtown, you juggle the railroad's greed, a cattle baron's private law, a gang wintering in the hills, and a saloon that hears everything first.

cards.prompt: The stage drops you at End-of-Track. Before your boots hit mud, Nellie the saloon owner offers a whiskey and 'a word about who really runs things.'

leftChoice.text: Buy a round for the whole house

rightChoice.text: Pin the badge on and get to work

cards.prompt: Nellie leans close over the bar. 'The railroad man rides in tomorrow. I know his weaknesses — for a price.'

rightChoice.text: Keep your distance from saloon talk

cards.prompt: Gunfire! The hill gang hits the bank at dawn and rides for the ridgeline with the miners' payroll.

leftChoice.text: Track them with a lean, fast posse

rightChoice.text: Hunker down and protect the town

cards.prompt: Colonel Vane, the cattle baron, rides in with twenty guns. 'Deputize my men, Sheriff, and this valley stays quiet.'

rightChoice.text: Refuse — the law answers to the town

cards.prompt: The telegraph boy sprints up, breathless. 'Railroad surveyor's coming with a court order and a hired gun!'

leftChoice.text: Prepare the town and check the paperwork

rightChoice.text: Let the company sort its own business

cards.prompt: Your grizzled deputy spits tobacco. 'That gang winters in the box canyon. We hit 'em now or we fort up here.'

rightChoice.text: Fortify the town and wait

cards.prompt: A homesteader and Vane's cowboys stand nose to nose over a fence line, hands near their belts.

rightChoice.text: Rule for the baron's men

cards.prompt: A fresh bounty poster names Cole Rourke, the gang's leader — five hundred in gold, dead or alive.

rightChoice.text: Sell what you know to freelance hunters

cards.prompt: The railroad man wagers a fat stack across the poker table, grinning through cigar smoke.

rightChoice.text: Play cautious and read his tells

cards.prompt: A bloodied kerchief pinned to your door: 'Talk, Sheriff, or bleed.' The gang wants a parley.

rightChoice.text: Refuse and prepare an ambush

cards.prompt: The confrontation comes to a head at the old ford. Rourke offers gold for a quiet winter in the hills.

leftChoice.text: Take the deal and look the other way

rightChoice.text: Spring the fight here and now

cards.prompt: In the chaos a lamp shatters and flames climb the dry timber of Front Street.

rightChoice.text: Save the jail and the county records

cards.prompt: The preacher plants his Bible on your desk. 'This town needs a moral spine, Sheriff. Fund the mission.'

leftChoice.text: Fund the church and Sunday order

rightChoice.text: Keep the law strictly secular

cards.prompt: The railroad demands the town commons for a depot yard. Townsfolk block the surveyors with shovels.

rightChoice.text: Stand with the townsfolk

cards.prompt: Silver-strike rumors draw claim-jumpers who feud over the same creek bend.

rightChoice.text: Seize the strike for the county coffers

cards.prompt: Vane's man delivers the ultimatum: 'The Colonel runs the valley. Ride under his brand or ride out.'

leftChoice.text: Stand your ground against the baron

rightChoice.text: Bend the knee for now

cards.prompt: With Vane emboldened, you need muscle fast. The town square waits for your call.

leftChoice.text: Arm the townsfolk as a militia

rightChoice.text: Hire hard-eyed mercenaries

cards.prompt: The night before the reckoning, you and your deputy lay out the plan by lamplight.

leftChoice.text: Rig a clever trap on Main Street

rightChoice.text: Call for a fair, public duel

cards.prompt: High noon. The clock tower tolls. Vane and Rourke stand in the dust of Main Street, waiting on you.

leftChoice.text: Walk out and face them yourself

rightChoice.text: Let your deputy take the shot

cards.prompt: The gunsmoke clears. The valley belongs to the law now, and End-of-Track will remember which way you chose to fall.

leftChoice.text: Hang up the badge and ride into the sunset

rightChoice.text: Stay on as the sheriff the town earned

name: Rain City
description: A private eye, a case that smells wrong, and a city where every streetlight hides a debt. Classic hard-boiled noir.
systemInstruction: You are a creative storyteller for the interactive fiction game "SwipeVerse". You create challenging scenarios themed as "Rain City". The stats are Nerve, The Retainer, Contacts, The Case.

deck.name: Rain City: The Kessler File
deck.description: A broke private eye takes a missing-husband case from a woman who lies, in a city where the cops owe favors they resent, the papers pay for secrets, and every truth is buried under rain.

cards.prompt: Neon bleeds through the rain on your office window when she walks in—Mrs. Kessler, all mink and trouble, saying her husband's vanished. Her hands don't shake like a worried wife's.

leftChoice.text: Take the retainer and the case.

rightChoice.text: Turn her away—something's rotten.

cards.prompt: Her cash sits in your drawer like a loaded gun. The husband's last known address is a fleabag hotel across town.

leftChoice.text: Set up a cold, wet stakeout.

rightChoice.text: Shake down your street contacts first.

cards.prompt: She leaves in a cloud of perfume, but a flat-faced goon lingers under the streetlamp, watching your door.

leftChoice.text: Slip out and tail the goon.

rightChoice.text: Kill the lights and lay low.

cards.prompt: The Alameda Hotel drips misery from every gutter. Room 4C stays dark, but the desk clerk keeps glancing your way.

leftChoice.text: Wait all night in the rain.

rightChoice.text: Slide the clerk a folded bill.

cards.prompt: Halloran of the Ledger corners you in a diner, cigarette bobbing. 'Word is you got a live one. Papers pay cash for a name.'

rightChoice.text: Keep your mouth shut.

cards.prompt: Lieutenant Brannigan owes you for the Delgado business, and he hates it. 'Ask your favor and get outta my precinct,' he growls.

leftChoice.text: Cash in the favor for the files.

rightChoice.text: Save the favor for a rainier day.

cards.prompt: Uniforms boot in the door of the Blue Parrot mid-question. A raid—and you're in the middle of it.

rightChoice.text: Flash the license, play it cool.

cards.prompt: The bartender wipes the same glass three times. 'Kessler? Never heard of him.' The wet ring on the bar says otherwise.

rightChoice.text: Buy a round and play friendly.

cards.prompt: A black sedan noses out of the alley and rolls slow through the downpour. No plates you can read from here—not yet.

leftChoice.text: Tail it down to the docks.

rightChoice.text: Catch the plate and back off.

cards.prompt: You find him at last—Kessler, face-down in a warehouse, two days dead. This was never about a runaway husband.

rightChoice.text: Search the body before the cops come.

cards.prompt: Two snitches, two stories. The dockworker swears he saw a woman; the showgirl swears she saw a badge.

rightChoice.text: Trust the showgirl.

cards.prompt: They're waiting in the alley—two of them with pipes. 'Drop the case, gumshoe,' one grunts as the rain runs pink.

rightChoice.text: Take the beating and listen.

cards.prompt: In Kessler's coat lining: a safe-deposit key, numbered 217. Somebody would kill to get it—maybe already did.

leftChoice.text: Open the box at the bank.

rightChoice.text: Sell the key to the highest bidder.

cards.prompt: Mrs. Kessler sweeps back in, tears on cue, with a brand-new story about her 'poor lost husband.' The math still doesn't add up.

leftChoice.text: Lay the truth on the table.

rightChoice.text: Play along and let her hang herself.

cards.prompt: District Attorney Voss sends a car for you. 'Drop the Kessler matter,' he purrs over brandy, 'and forget you ever heard my name.'

leftChoice.text: Tell him where to stuff it.

rightChoice.text: Bargain for protection instead.

cards.prompt: You've got the crooked official cold—ledgers, payoffs, the works. He mops his brow across the desk from you.

leftChoice.text: Threaten to blow it all open.

rightChoice.text: Pocket his bribe and walk.

cards.prompt: The real killer bolts through the storm toward the pier. Your lungs burn and your gun's slick with rain.

leftChoice.text: Corner him at the pier's end.

rightChoice.text: Whistle up Brannigan for backup.

cards.prompt: Sirens. A planted pistol, your prints, a corpse you never touched. Voss has framed you neat as a Sunday suit.

leftChoice.text: Run and dig up the proof.

rightChoice.text: Surrender and trust the truth.

cards.prompt: It all comes down to one rain-lashed room: the widow, the DA, the lieutenant, and the file that ties them together.

leftChoice.text: Lay the whole rotten scheme bare.

rightChoice.text: Cut a quiet deal and let it slide.

cards.prompt: Dawn finally cracks grey over the wet city. The Kessler file is closed—one way or another—and the rain, at last, begins to ease.

leftChoice.text: Pour a drink and let the case rest.

rightChoice.text: Hang out the shingle for the next lie.

name: The Ledger
description: The business world. Deck 1 — Runway: twelve months of cash, one company, no safety net. Every card is a real founder's trade-off.
systemInstruction: You are a creative storyteller for the interactive fiction game "SwipeVerse". You create challenging scenarios themed as "The Ledger". The stats are Control, Runway, The Team, Insight.

deck.name: Runway
deck.description: Bootstrap a software startup through twelve months of runway, trading founder control, cash, team trust, and hard-won business insight on the road to product-market fit.

cards.prompt: You've quit your job with twelve months of savings. Before writing a line of code, you must decide how to capitalize the company. Cash buys speed, but every dollar raised costs a slice of ownership.

rightChoice.text: Bootstrap on your own savings

cards.prompt: Your first product is ready. Do you price low to win market share fast, or price on the value delivered? Cheap prices win logos but starve margins; premium prices fund the business but slow adoption.

rightChoice.text: Value-based premium pricing

cards.prompt: Growth is slow. Your instinct is to buy attention, but your product still has rough edges. Spending on marketing an unfinished product just amplifies churn — the opportunity cost is the fix you didn't ship.

rightChoice.text: Polish the product before scaling spend

cards.prompt: A VC hands you a term sheet: real cash, but two board seats and a liquidation preference. It extends your runway dramatically at the cost of the control you'll never fully win back.

leftChoice.text: Sign for the full round and board seats

rightChoice.text: Negotiate a smaller round, keep control

cards.prompt: Your co-founder demands a 50/50 equity split even though you're carrying more of the load. Equal splits build trust but ignore contribution; a weighted split with vesting is fairer but risks resentment.

rightChoice.text: Insist on a weighted split with vesting

cards.prompt: You can afford one senior manager or two junior hires. The senior brings judgment and needs no hand-holding; the juniors add capacity and hunger but require mentoring you can't spare.

rightChoice.text: Hire two eager juniors

cards.prompt: Your biggest customer offers a fat contract — but demands exclusivity, locking you out of their competitors. The revenue is real, yet customer-concentration risk means one client could soon own your fate.

rightChoice.text: Decline, keep your pipeline diverse

cards.prompt: Six months in, usage is flat and retention is weak. Do you pivot toward the one feature customers actually love, or persevere on the original vision you still believe in?

leftChoice.text: Pivot to what the data shows

rightChoice.text: Persevere and double down

cards.prompt: You're profitable on paper, but customers pay net-90 while payroll is due Friday. Profit isn't cash — and cash is what keeps the lights on. A factor will advance you money for a fee.

leftChoice.text: Factor receivables to free up cash

rightChoice.text: Hold firm and wait for payment

cards.prompt: Your team debates the funnel: launch a freemium tier to maximize signups, or gate everything behind a paid trial. Free users teach you loads about the product but burn cash and may never convert.

rightChoice.text: Require a paid trial from day one

cards.prompt: A mentor reviews your books and warns your burn rate leaves only four months of runway. You can cut costs to survive, or keep spending to hit the growth numbers investors want to see.

leftChoice.text: Cut costs, including a small layoff

rightChoice.text: Keep spending to chase growth

cards.prompt: An angel offers a convertible note on generous terms. It buys breathing room now, but the discount and cap mean more dilution when it converts at your next round.

rightChoice.text: Stay lean and skip the money

cards.prompt: A rival poaches your lead engineer with a huge offer. You can counter with a raise and more equity — draining runway — or let them go and redistribute the work across the team.

rightChoice.text: Let them leave, redistribute the load

cards.prompt: A whale customer will renew — if you build a custom feature that isn't on your roadmap. Bespoke work pays the bills but fragments the product and steals focus from your core users.

rightChoice.text: Hold the line on your roadmap

cards.prompt: A major tech publication offers a splashy launch feature. It could send a flood of traffic — or expose your infrastructure and reputation to a wave your servers can't handle.

leftChoice.text: Go for the big, risky launch

rightChoice.text: Run a slow, controlled beta

cards.prompt: An established player offers an acquihire: a comfortable exit and jobs for your team. It's safety and a return, but you'd surrender the company you set out to build.

rightChoice.text: Reject it and keep building

cards.prompt: Sales are trickling in and you're tempted to hire a whole sales team to scale. But premature scaling before real product-market fit is a classic startup killer — you'd be pouring fuel on an unproven engine.

rightChoice.text: Nail product-market fit first

cards.prompt: The bank account is nearly empty. New investors will only fund a down round at a lower valuation; your existing backers offer a smaller bridge loan instead. Both dilute morale and control.

rightChoice.text: Take the insider bridge loan

cards.prompt: With traction proven, a Series A term sheet lands. Take the big check for hypergrowth and heavy dilution, or raise a modest round and build toward sustainable, profitable growth.

leftChoice.text: Take the large Series A for hypergrowth

rightChoice.text: Raise modestly, grow sustainably

cards.prompt: Twelve months on, your startup has outlived its original runway. You've learned the hard truths: profit isn't cash, dilution is forever, customer concentration is fragility, and product-market fit is everything. The gambit paid off — the company stands on its own.

leftChoice.text: Toast the team and plan the next chapter

rightChoice.text: Write down the lessons for the next founder

name: The Perimeter
description: The security world. Deck 1 — Zero Hour: run security for a company under live attack. Contain or investigate, patch or keep uptime, disclose or dig deeper.
systemInstruction: You are a creative storyteller for the interactive fiction game "SwipeVerse". You create challenging scenarios themed as "The Perimeter". The stats are Clearance, Budget, Trust, Threat Intel.

deck.name: Zero Hour
deck.description: As a mid-size company's new security lead, steer a single escalating incident from a suspicious phishing email through lateral movement, ransomware detonation, and the messy aftermath — balancing your team's authority, budget, organizational trust, and threat intelligence.

cards.prompt: Day one. An accountant forwards a slick invoice email with a login link that doesn't match your domain. Others may have clicked. What's your first move?

leftChoice.text: Isolate the account, pull the mail logs, and hunt for who clicked

rightChoice.text: Blast a company-wide phishing warning and quietly monitor

cards.prompt: Your scanner flags a critical remote-code-execution vuln on the app server. A patch exists, but the box runs production. Patch or wait?

leftChoice.text: Patch now and eat the downtime — the exposure is too dangerous

rightChoice.text: Schedule it for the maintenance window three days out

cards.prompt: You have leftover budget for exactly one thing: a third-party penetration test to find gaps, or an EDR tool for continuous endpoint visibility.

leftChoice.text: Commission the pentest — find the holes before attackers do

rightChoice.text: Deploy the EDR — you need eyes on every endpoint now

cards.prompt: Logs suggest a set of credentials was harvested. Resetting everyone's password is safe but will bury the helpdesk in tickets.

leftChoice.text: Force an org-wide password reset immediately

rightChoice.text: Reset only the accounts you can tie to the phish

cards.prompt: An access review shows a shared service account with domain-admin rights used by half the ops team — a least-privilege nightmare. How aggressively do you fix it?

leftChoice.text: Revoke and re-scope everything today, business be damned

rightChoice.text: Roll out a phased de-privileging plan with owners

cards.prompt: Your EDR lights up: the intruder is moving laterally, hopping from the accountant's laptop toward a file server. Contain or observe?

leftChoice.text: Segment the network and cut them off now

rightChoice.text: Let them run in a watched segment to learn their playbook

cards.prompt: A vendor offers a premium threat-intelligence feed with fresh IOCs matching this actor. Your open-source feeds are free but slower.

leftChoice.text: Buy the premium feed — attribution and IOCs matter now

rightChoice.text: Stick with open-source intel and community sharing

cards.prompt: You want MFA everywhere, but managers complain it slows logins and floods you with 'lost phone' requests. How far do you push?

leftChoice.text: Mandate MFA on every account, no exceptions

rightChoice.text: Require it only on privileged and remote accounts for now

cards.prompt: Evidence of a breach is mounting. The CEO wants it kept quiet until you 'know for sure.' Legal risk and customer trust hang in the balance.

leftChoice.text: Disclose early to leadership and prep customer notice

rightChoice.text: Investigate quietly and disclose only once confirmed

cards.prompt: Word of the incident has reached a regulator and a nervous customer. How you communicate now shapes everything.

leftChoice.text: Full transparency: timeline, scope, remediation steps

rightChoice.text: Issue a tight, lawyer-approved minimal statement

cards.prompt: Before things get worse, your backup admin swears the nightly backups are solid. But nobody has done a test restore in months.

leftChoice.text: Run a full test restore and verify backup integrity offline

rightChoice.text: Trust the green dashboard and move on

cards.prompt: Too late to prevent it: ransomware detonates. Files across shares are encrypting in real time and a ransom note appears. Every second counts.

leftChoice.text: Pull the plug — power down and disconnect everything now

rightChoice.text: Isolate but keep systems live to preserve forensic evidence

cards.prompt: The operators open a chat: pay in crypto for the decryptor, or lose the data and see it leaked. Your backups are uncertain.

leftChoice.text: Negotiate and pay — you can't risk the leak or downtime

rightChoice.text: Refuse to pay and commit to recovery on your own terms

cards.prompt: Recovery time. Your backups predate the intrusion by weeks — but the attacker may have been dormant inside them. Restore or rebuild?

leftChoice.text: Restore from backups after scanning them in a clean-room

rightChoice.text: Rebuild critical systems from known-good images from scratch

cards.prompt: Forensics confirm customer PII was exfiltrated. Breach-notification clocks are ticking. How and when do you disclose to those affected?

leftChoice.text: Notify affected parties fully and promptly with guidance

rightChoice.text: Delay notification until the investigation is airtight

cards.prompt: The board convenes an emergency session. They want accountability — and to know whether to fund security properly or cut losses.

leftChoice.text: Make the case for a real security budget and mandate

rightChoice.text: Promise to do more with less and rebuild goodwill

cards.prompt: A log detail nags you: the initial access looks like someone knew exactly where to look. Could this be an insider?

leftChoice.text: Quietly pull that employee's activity trail yourself

rightChoice.text: Loop in HR and legal before touching anything

cards.prompt: The dust is settling and you're cleared to build a real capability. Stand up an in-house SOC, or contract a managed security provider?

leftChoice.text: Hire and train an in-house team you control

rightChoice.text: Sign an MSSP for 24/7 monitoring and expertise

cards.prompt: The post-incident review lands on your desk. You can push a layered defense-in-depth roadmap, or just close the specific gaps that were exploited.

leftChoice.text: Commit to a defense-in-depth program with segmentation, logging, and drills

rightChoice.text: Patch the exact holes used and call it done

cards.prompt: Six months later, the company is still standing — and hardened by hard lessons. As you write the retrospective, what note do you leave for your successor?

leftChoice.text: 'Security is a process, not a purchase — assume breach and keep testing.'

rightChoice.text: 'Trust your instincts, but prove them with evidence and least privilege.'

name: Merge Conflict
description: Lead a dev team from prototype to production. Tech debt, testing, rewrites and deadlines — the judgment calls that make or break software.
systemInstruction: You are a creative storyteller for the interactive fiction game "SwipeVerse". You create challenging scenarios themed as "Merge Conflict". The stats are Influence, Deadline, The Team, Code Quality.

deck.name: Merge Conflict: From Prototype to Production
deck.description: As the new tech lead of a scrappy team, every swipe trades your influence, your deadline slack, your team's health, and the codebase's quality on the road from demo to launch.

cards.prompt: Day one as tech lead. The prototype works in the demo but the repo is a minefield of TODOs and no tests. The founder wants a launch date by Friday.

rightChoice.text: Spend a week auditing the codebase first

cards.prompt: You're choosing the foundation. A shiny reactive framework promises speed and hiring buzz; the boring proven stack your team already knows just works.

rightChoice.text: Stick with the boring proven stack

cards.prompt: During the investor demo, the prototype crashes on a null it never hit before. Every eye turns to you in the room.

leftChoice.text: Duct-tape a live hotfix on the spot

rightChoice.text: Own the failure and reschedule the demo

cards.prompt: The PM wants a flashy feature for the sales demo. You can fake it with hardcoded shortcuts now, or build it properly and slip the date.

leftChoice.text: Take the tech debt, ship the demo

rightChoice.text: Slip the date, keep it clean

cards.prompt: A senior dev wants to shard the database and add caching layers 'for scale' before you have a single paying user. She warns of bottlenecks; you smell premature optimization.

rightChoice.text: Ship simple, optimize once you can measure

cards.prompt: The deadline screams and PRs are piling up unreviewed. Mandating review slows everyone; skipping it lets bugs slip straight to main.

rightChoice.text: Waive reviews to hit the date

cards.prompt: QA blocks the release: coverage is near zero and regressions keep resurfacing. Building a real test suite costs days you don't have.

leftChoice.text: Invest in the test suite now

rightChoice.text: Defer tests, plan to firefight later

cards.prompt: The legacy payments module is a tangle nobody fully understands. You can rewrite it from scratch or wrap it and strangle it piece by piece.

rightChoice.text: Strangler pattern, migrate incrementally

cards.prompt: Two engineers are deadlocked: one wants a quick pragmatic patch, the other insists on a clean abstraction. The debate is stalling the sprint.

leftChoice.text: Side with the pragmatic quick fix

rightChoice.text: Side with the careful abstraction

cards.prompt: You need authentication. A battle-tested SaaS provider costs money but ships tomorrow; building it in-house gives control but eats weeks.

rightChoice.text: Build auth in-house

cards.prompt: Only one engineer understands the billing engine — a bus factor of one. Pairing a junior with him would slow delivery but spread the knowledge.

leftChoice.text: Pair the junior in to spread knowledge

rightChoice.text: Keep the expert siloed and moving fast

cards.prompt: 2am. Production is down and users are locked out. You can push a targeted hotfix nobody has reviewed, or roll back to the last known-good release and lose today's changes.

rightChoice.text: Roll back to the last good release

cards.prompt: An engineer points out you have no dashboards or alerts — you only learn of outages from angry tweets. Observability work is invisible to stakeholders.

leftChoice.text: Invest in monitoring and tracing now

rightChoice.text: Fly blind, bolt on logging later

cards.prompt: Code freeze is in an hour and a giant feature branch is waiting. Merging it now risks a nasty regression; splitting it into small PRs means missing the freeze.

leftChoice.text: Merge the giant branch and pray

rightChoice.text: Split it into small, safe PRs

cards.prompt: A key stakeholder 'just needs one more feature' before launch — the classic scope creep. Saying yes torches your timeline; saying no risks your standing.

rightChoice.text: Say no and protect the scope

cards.prompt: A security researcher discloses an injection vulnerability days before launch. A proper fix needs a redesign; a quick input filter mitigates it for now.

leftChoice.text: Delay launch to patch it properly

rightChoice.text: Ship with a quick mitigation

cards.prompt: After the messy incident, leadership wants a name attached to the failure. Your team is watching how you'll handle accountability.

rightChoice.text: Assign blame publicly to satisfy the top

cards.prompt: Cutover day approaches. You can roll out gradually behind a canary and feature flags, or do a single big-bang switch to the new system.

rightChoice.text: Big-bang cutover overnight

cards.prompt: Your most reliable engineer is visibly burnt out, working weekends to hit launch. Pulling them off now slows you; pushing them risks losing them for good.

leftChoice.text: Give them time off, redistribute load

rightChoice.text: Push everyone through to launch

cards.prompt: Launch day. The pipeline is green, the team is gathered, and the deploy button is under your finger. How you go live sets the tone for everything after.

leftChoice.text: Launch quietly and watch the dashboards

rightChoice.text: Launch loud with a public announcement

name: The Defender's Dilemma
description: Day one as the sole security lead at a small business. Every choice trades safety, productivity, budget and trust against each other — push any meter too far and you lose. A five-minute 'assume breach' icebreaker: you can't win, only balance.
systemInstruction: You are a creative storyteller for the interactive fiction game "SwipeVerse". You create challenging scenarios themed as "The Defender's Dilemma". The stats are Security, Budget, Trust, Productivity.

deck.name: The Defender's Dilemma
deck.description: You are the brand-new, only security person at a 40-staff Aussie business on a shoestring, and every hallway conversation this week is a trade-off with no clean answer.

cards.prompt: Day one, 9am. Office manager Priya walks you to the front desk. 'Everyone uses the same login here, and the password's on this sticky note so no one forgets. It just works, love. You're not going to change that on your first morning, are you?'

leftChoice.text: Leave it for now, plan a proper fix

rightChoice.text: Ban shared logins immediately

cards.prompt: CEO Dave pokes his head in. 'New sales hire starts in an hour. Just give her access to everything so she can hit the ground running — no need for all the checking-people-out business. We're a small mob, we trust each other.'

leftChoice.text: Do a quick basic check first

rightChoice.text: Grant full access to keep Dave happy

cards.prompt: IT junior Sam shows you a list. 'These admin accounts belong to people who left ages ago — but they still work. I wasn't sure if we're allowed to touch them, so I left them alone.'

rightChoice.text: Leave them, someone might need them

cards.prompt: Sales lead Marco corners you. 'I close deals from my personal Gmail — it's fast and clients love it. The company email is clunky and I don't have time to relearn everything this quarter.'

rightChoice.text: Move him onto company email now

cards.prompt: Warehouse worker Kev holds up a USB stick. 'Found this in the car park! Might belong to a customer. I'll just pop it into that spare laptop and see whose it is, hey?'

leftChoice.text: Quarantine it, don't plug it in

rightChoice.text: Let him plug it in to help find the owner

cards.prompt: A vendor named Terry offers a cheap antivirus subscription. 'It's basic, but it's better than nothing, and it won't blow your tiny budget. Sign today and I'll knock off ten percent.'

rightChoice.text: Decline and save the money

cards.prompt: Auditor Lena arrives for a scheduled review. 'I need your security documentation — policies, records, that sort of thing.' You've been here four days and have almost none of it.

leftChoice.text: Be honest and admit the gaps

rightChoice.text: Talk it up to look organised

cards.prompt: Friday, 4:45pm. Journalist Jordan rings. 'I've heard your company may have leaked customer details. I'm filing in an hour — got a comment before the weekend?' Your whole team is halfway out the door.

rightChoice.text: Say 'no comment' and stall

cards.prompt: Customer Mrs Chen emails: 'Where do you keep my details, and is it actually safe? My neighbour got scammed and I'm nervous.'

rightChoice.text: Reassure her it's totally safe

cards.prompt: An email pretending to be Dave lands in a dozen inboxes: 'Urgent — buy $500 of gift cards and send me the codes.' It's a scam. Sam asks whether to warn everyone or keep it quiet.

rightChoice.text: Handle it quietly, don't alarm folk

cards.prompt: Bad news: receptionist Bec already typed her password into the scam site before you caught it. Sam looks at you nervously.

rightChoice.text: Assume it's fine and just watch

cards.prompt: Sam whispers a chilling fact: there are no backups of anything. 'If ransomware locks our files, we lose the lot. But proper backups aren't free, and you know how Dave feels about spending.'

rightChoice.text: Skip it for now and hope

cards.prompt: You draft a password rule. Word spreads and staff groan. Team lead Aisha says, 'If you make it too painful, people just write them down or find sneaky workarounds.'

rightChoice.text: Demand long passwords, changed monthly

cards.prompt: Sam pitches MFA — a one-time code on your phone when you log in. 'It stops most stolen-password attacks. But people will grumble about the extra step every morning.'

rightChoice.text: Postpone it, too much hassle now

cards.prompt: Visitors keep asking for the Wi-Fi password — which is the same network your staff and systems use. Priya just hands it out to whoever asks.

leftChoice.text: Set up a separate guest Wi-Fi

rightChoice.text: Keep sharing the main Wi-Fi

cards.prompt: Aisha returns, worried. 'Morale's dropping. People are quietly using their own apps and cloud tools to dodge the new rules — shadow IT, you called it. Ease up before it gets worse?'

leftChoice.text: Ease off on a few controls

rightChoice.text: Hold firm on every control

cards.prompt: Dave wants a chat about money. 'I reckon we can trim your budget — nothing bad's happened yet, right?' You'd rather invest in the basics you still lack.

rightChoice.text: Cut spending to please Dave

cards.prompt: A staff laptop is nicked from a parked car. Sam asks, 'Do we trigger the remote wipe? The drive's encrypted, so the thief probably can't read it — but wiping means we lose whatever wasn't backed up.'

rightChoice.text: Do nothing and hope it's fine

cards.prompt: End of week one. Dave asks you to brief the board. Do you promise them ironclad safety, or tell the honest truth — that no one is unbreachable and the real work is being ready when something slips through?

rightChoice.text: Promise the board you're bulletproof

cards.prompt: Week one is done. You didn't lock the place into a fortress, you didn't leave the doors flung open, and you didn't blow the budget or hoard it. Some things will still go wrong — and now the team's ready to cope when they do. That balance, not perfection, is the job. Welcome aboard.

leftChoice.text: Pour a Friday drink and start week two

rightChoice.text: Jot down what to fix next Monday
